feedback.text: When it comes to digital fairness, can anything be more important than customers actually being capable of actually owning the very digital copies of things they purchase? Of course not. And yet, a lot of companies within the video game industry voluntarily choose to actually destroy video games. Many actively and completely remove the ability from paying customers to play a video game that they bought despite not being required to do so in any way, primarily because of predatory anti-consumer practices, dark patterns, misleading marketing, and exploitation of the customers with the fear of missing out. Companies actively choose to destroy the video games of their customers and not include any end-of-life plan at all because if the user cannot actually play the older version of the video game from before, there is a higher chance to push the customer hard towards influencing and pushing him to have to play the new, similar, more predatory, and more heavily monetized game from that company, since their previous game is now permanently gone, unable to "steal" the attention of the customer away from the new and shiny game that the company wants to profit with. Video game companies are free to remove their support from any of their creations at any time. But the problem is when those game companies actively choose to leave those video games in a non-playable state in the end. A lot of games from the past have not been so malicious towards their customers and have allowed those customers to host their own video game servers through the providing of game server binary files, peer-to-peer local network connectivity, or even the very source code to the game when it comes to some very good companies and company leaders who understand the value of happy customers owning what they paid for and also understand and love video game history preservation and archival so that these experiences can be enjoyed by all of the future people in this world for centuries to come, instead of being actively destroyed for short-term profit through predatory anti-consumer practices. I request that you also join in and support the fight for these proper consumer ownership rights of consumers owning the copies of the video games they bought and protect those customers from companies that needlessly destroy that consumer-bought digital content. The best way to do so right now is to collaborate with the Official European Citizens' Initiative "Stop Destroying Videogames" which at the time of writing has gone a lot over the initial goal and acquired over 1,438,805 signatures just from people from the EU countries themselves, and garnered many more millions of supporters throughout the entire world.
